So previously, we described that how we can build different kind of computer vision models to perform different kind of tasks, such as to perform image classification, to perform object detection, to perform segmentation, et cetera. And for majority of these cases, we need to define a loss function. using our training data. The training data normally contains some images with some human annotation for these images. We use these human annotations to define the loss function so that we can train these machine learning models.

In today's lecture, we're going to describe different kinds of ideas that how can we train these machine learning models without any human annotation. And this is normally called self-supervised learning. So although that we no longer need any labels provided by humans, we still need to design some signals to supervise the training of the model. Because you can see that all these models, they are driven by the loss function. So the loss function describes maybe the difference between some model prediction and human annotation.

It can also be describing some prediction model to signals not defined by human, but from the data itself. And we're going to introduce three ways how we can design the required supervisory signals. And these three types of self-supervised learning methods are called either pretext-task-based methods, or contrast-learning-based methods, and the generative model-based methods.

So for the first category, pretext task-based methods, we need to define a task for the model to learn something. And there are different ways you can design these tasks. And the most easy way is to ask the model to predict relative positions in the images. For example, that if we provide two image patches to the model, patch one and patch two here, whether the model can tell us that the relative position of patch two compared to patch one, which means that if we put patch one in the center of this grid, where should we put patch two?

So it's a bit like you are playing some jigsaw game that how can you figure out where to put different jigsaw pieces. And for this, maybe this patch two represents one of the ears of the cat and that the model would need to figure out which would put patch two at the top left corner. And this is called the relative position between two patches, and this can be used to train the machine learning model.

And if we draw these nine different positions on the 2D space, you can see when we put patch one in the middle, there are eight possible positions for patch two. And that means that when you ask the network to predict this relative position, it can be formulated as a classification problem where you have eight labels. Each label corresponds to one of these locations. And here that when we define this task that the each of these labels or the location patch two compared to patch one does not require any human annotations input basically so we just need to take a full image and from the full image you can write a few lines of code to extract patch two and patch one and also to determine the location the relative position of patch one compared to patch relative position of patch two compared to patch one And learning such a task would force the model to understand, first, to understand some features for this cat.

Second, to understand that for different parts of the cat, how should these different parts relate to each other. So we hope that although this is a very simple task, this can force the model to learn some useful features to perform image classification. So when we formulate this as a machine learning problem, the input to the machine learning model X contains the patch one and patch two. And output of this machine learning model is denoted as Y here.

Y is one of the eight possible positions. And in terms of machine learning model, to perform this task, it can be any model. For example, we can use this AlexNet model. And when we look at the different layers of AlexNet model, it starts with some convolution layers, some max pooling layers. And at the end, there are a few fully connected layers, which reduce all the information to certain neurons, such as 1,000 neurons. And when we analyze this network, we assume that the majority part of this network is focused on extracting features from the input image.

And the last few layers of this network is focused on performing certain tasks. And for this case, the original task is to perform image classification for 1,000 different classes. So that is why at the end, we need to design these 1,000 neurons here. And for the previous layer, they are just used for extracting features for the input image. And we want to see whether we can use this relative position as a task to pre-train all these layers within the red box.

For example, we just move all the layers in this red box to the same red box in this diagram. And here that we still that within the red box, everything is the same. It's just we need to modify the input and output of this red box. So for the input, now we have two input, patch one and patch two. So both of these patches can go through these convolution layers to generate some features. And although there are two inputs, but we still want to use just one convolution network, which means that on the left, this red box on the left is the same of all the layers on the right.

This is also called as weight sharing. So when you use PyTorch to develop some networks, even if you develop two branches of convolution layers, you can set these two branches to share the same weight or same parameters. And that is how we modify the input. Now the input becomes two patches. And we also need to modify the output. So previously, we were using the output to perform a classification for 1,000 different classes.

And this time, we just need to perform a classification for eight different positions. So you can see for the last layer here, we put eight neurons over there to perform this eight-way classification task. And then between the red box and to that eight neurons, we put some fully connect layers, which are these FC7 and FC8 layers. And after you build a network like this, then you can provide, you can just like take an input image, extract two patches, provide them input, and also ask this network to predict the relative position as output.

And all of these networks can be trained without any human annotation. So this part within the red box can be trained using self-supervised learning or using this relative position prediction task. At the end, how can we use this pre-trained network It depends on what kind of task you want to perform. If your task is still to perform classification for 1,000 different classes, that means that although everything within the red box is pre-trained, we still need to add these two last layers.

And for the last layer, we need to put 1,000 neurons there. And also for these two last layers, we don't know their parameter because they have not been pre-trained. And when we combine everything pre-trained within Redbox with the last two layers, we need to do some further training to make sure that these two last layers also get some good parameters to perform these image classification tasks for 1,000 different classes. And this part will be trained later.

And when we train the last two layers, because we need to define the loss function for these 1,000 neurons, that means we still need to use some training set. And this training set still needs some human annotation. So why do we still bother to do this if at the end we still need to use some human annotation? The advantage is that for the majority part of this network, we already get good parameters. We only need to, like, optimize the parameter for these last two layers, and that may mean that we don't need many human annotations.

To train these two layers, we can just use a relatively small data set, and maybe you just ask some human annotators to annotate one hour, you can create all the training data for these last two layers, and this can reduce the cost of human annotation. So this is one way we can pre-train the network using a relative position prediction as a task. And we're going to introduce another task. Any questions?

When we want to apply this pre-trained network to some new tasks, there are two ways we can do the training. One way is keep the red box frozen, freeze all the primates over there, just to train the last two layers. And this is called linear probing because the last layers, they are linear layers. We just need to probe some good primates for those two layers, linear probing. The other way is... train all the layers.

This is called fine-tuning, because we already get some good parameters, we just need to move them a little bit, so that's why it's called fine-tuning. How do we decide how fit we have this, should we?

Yes. So there are different ways to determine what will be the best patch size. Of course, the most easiest way is you just run a few different experiments with different patch size to see which one performs better. So in this way, you get some... good patch size by empirical experiments. The other way is that you can think about what your convolution network looks like.

Here we have all the layers within the red box, and there are certain layers that will perform down sampling for your input image or input patches. For example, that here, these convolution layer 1, it comes with stride equals 4, performs downsampling by a factor of 4. We also have the pooling layer 1, pooling layer 2, pooling layer 3. Each of these pooling layer will also downsample the image by a factor of 2.

So at the end, in total, this will be downsampled by a factor of 32. That means that if you use any patch which is smaller than 32 by 32, the final feature you get is just one feature bracket describing everything. So we want to use some patch which may be bigger than 32 by 32. So at the end, you still get some reasonable features from these input patches.

So we call all of these human design tasks as a pretext task. And apart from relative position prediction, another common way to define the pretext task is to use examples or exemplars. And here that we regard each input image in our dataset as one exemplar. And each one exemplar is one class. And then we apply augmentation to this example image to create image for the same class.

Suppose that we start from some data set where we don't have any human annotations. And this is our data set. You can see there are 24 images. And we just pick one of these images within this red box. And then we perform data augmentation. You can add some intensity noise, or you can rotate this image. You can change some color of this image, but we assume that after augmentation, all of this image still belong to the same class.

And in this case, this is a class for the deer. And if we perform augmentation for k times, then we'll generate one class that contains K different training images. This is just for one input image in our original data set. And in our original data set, we can do the same for every of these individual image. For example, we take another image, which is within this green box.

and we perform augmentation to generate big K versions of the same image, they all belong to the same class. And if the original data set contains 24 images, that means at the end, we can create 24 different classes. and that will give us the label so at the end we can still uh take some alex net and ask some we take the majority of the alex net add a few more fully connected layers and for the last fully connected layers we put 24 neurons over there to perform this 24-way classification task okay And so this is a very interesting idea, but it also comes with limitations.

You can see that if our original dataset already contains one million images, at the end, we need to create one million different classes. And that means that for your final layer in your neural network, it can be very heavy and expensive. But it also provides some useful insights to our problem. So here, the reason we thought we can create different classes from the data is because there is a difference in the data.

So each image is different from the other. This difference is also called contrast. And we can leverage this kind of difference, contrast, or similarities within the data to design some task. And so using example as a pretext task was developed in year 2014.

And a few years after that, contrastive learning, or using the contrast to design these tasks becomes more popular. And in year 2020, there was a paper published, which is called SimCLR. And it is one of the first few works exploring this contrast for learning ideas. Basically, we just need to define the contrast or difference in the data. And the contrast will become our last function.

So how do we define the contrast in our data? So we assume that we can take some sample X from our data set. Then we can create two versions or two views of the same data. And this is similar to what we do data augmentation in the previous method. So take this input image. We create, maybe we rotate it by five degrees, create the first view of the image, denoted XI.

Then we translate the same input image by 10 pixel. We create another view of the same input image, denoted as XJ. Because they come from the same input image, they should be quite similar to each other. So we can define some loss function to encourage xi and xj to generate similar features. So what we do is that we just put some network here.

This red network can take xi as input, then generate zi as a feature for xi. And on the right hand side, we apply the same network, generate the feature Zj for input image Xj. And then we define some loss function to maximize the similarity between Zi and Zj. Yeah, so that is a high level idea.

And in engineering implementation, it is quite often this red box is split into two parts. The first part is called this uh function f this is called the encoder this function f can be alexnet or some other network it extract image feature and then there is also a very small projection head which is this function g essentially it is just a two layered fully connect layer or two layered multi-layer perception so this function uh So basically, maybe this part is F, and then you also have two more layers as G.

And the reason that this red box is split in two parts is that just in experiments, researchers have found out that adding these two layers projection head make everything perform better. OK. You may wonder, OK, here that we are only utilizing the similarity between images. We're only using one data sample. So where does the difference or contrast come from?

So we can, to define the difference, we can take another sample. So here that X and Y denotes two images drawn from the data set. They are different. And for this image Y, we also apply some maybe AlexNet or some other network to generate this feature z. And between the features z coming from image y and the features maybe zj or zi coming from this image x, they should be different.

So we want to define the loss function to minimize the similarity between z and zj here. So here you can see that when we define loss function, there are two parts we need to consider. One part is this blue part. It's about similarity. And the other part is this brown part. It is about the contrast. And just to show a more intuitive idea that we can use this diagram to show what contrastive learning looks like, we can take the image of a dog and then create two versions of the dog, put them into some convolution network, then ask the features generated by this convolution network to be similar.

We know that we want to maximize the similarity between z i and z j, because they come from the same image. And we also want to minimize the similarity between z i and other z, because they come from two different images. So a very natural idea is that we just write down a fraction. At the top, it's a similarity between zi and zj. And at the bottom, it's a similarity between zi and some other z. and this denominator will discourage you or force Zi to be moved away from that. So this is the rough idea, but also that when we implement it, it's slightly more complicated. Remember that here that we want to learn features from unannotated images and maybe that our dataset is quite large.

So we would regard the pairs of three and four as a positive pair. They should be similar. And we also use the sample 3 to form other pairs, such as we form a pair between 2 and 3. This should be different. And we regard the pair 2, 3 as a negative pair. And when you look at this table, you can see that there is only a single positive pair, which is between 3 and 4. And when you use 3 to form a pair with all the other numbers here, they are all negative pairs.

So here that we can define one positive pair and many negative pairs. And given these positive pairs and negative pairs, because there are two classes here, positive versus negative, then we can define a binary classification problem. And for binary classification problem, we can define the cross entropy loss. And the loss function for one positive pair, i and j, is defined in this way.

So you can see that it's quite similar to the cross entropy loss. So the inner product is if we have some if we live on the 2D space and we have two vectors, u and v, if both of these vectors have been normalized to become a unit vector, which means the length of this vector is one, then when you calculate that in the product, the inner product is equal to cosine theta.

Theta is the angle between u and v. And when you look at the definition for the similarity here, that for this part, we normalize u. And for this part, we normalize v so that they become unit vector. And there are two ways that we can use this encoder. One way is called linear probing. In linear probing, we phrase the encoder parameters. Then we add a linear classification head. which essentially is just one or two fully connected layers. And then we only train these last two layers on some training set to evaluate its performance on the test set.

And if we do this for this contrasting method, this figure reports its image classification accuracy for this linear probing process. So you can see that it's comparing the SimCLR method to many other self-supervised learning methods. And at least in this plot, it shows that SimCLR can achieve very good pre-trained image encoder. The other way that we don't freeze anything, we still add a classification head.

And this time, we train all the layers in this network. This is called fine tuning. You can imagine that in fine tuning, we have more freedom or flexibility to adjust these network parameters. So normally after fine tuning, the performance is higher than just linear probing. And this table reports on performance after fine tuning.

And in fine-tuning, for both linear probing and fine-tuning, we need to introduce some human annotation. But we hope that the cost for this human annotation is small. And in this table, you can see that compared to perform supervised learning using a very large annotated dataset, here using contrast learning or self-supervised learning, at the end, you only need to use maybe 1% of the annotation data set.

And you can still achieve a very high classification accuracy. So this is one of the first papers on contrast learning. So genitive model, these ideas actually come from natural language processing. And in natural language processing, one famous example is called GPT, standing for Genitive Pre-trained Transformer. So you can see the first term means that this is a generative method. The second term means that this is a way to pre-training a method or perform self-supervised learning. So how does the GPT perform the pre-training?

So it's basically define the task as this. which is to predict the next code token. So this is pre-training stage for GPT or other large language model. So we'll only talk about how GPT performs pre-training. So it defined a task called predict the next token. So here, in this diagram, the bottom shows the input and the top shows the output. And what it does, it's telling this language model, when you see me, you can start predicting the next token. And the language model would predict W. We just need to take a sentence from any book. Maybe in this book, it says that here we define a self-supervised learning task. And we just provide these words to the input to this model. In images, we have at least two dimensional and also that we have no strict order that which image patch should be in the front and which is in the back. And one idea which was being explored is called BERT, standing for bidirectional encoder representation from transformer. And using more straightforward words, BERT is essentially predicting the missing token. if we have some input I like playing something in the something and there are two missing tokens here we put special tokens here called mask mask and then we assume that we can develop some model to pre to take the features of the input words and also the mask token After some processing, after maybe some transformer models, self-attention layers, it can predict these missing tokens to be football and field.

So when we formulate this task, again, it does not involve any human annotation. We can just take any sentence from a book, and then we randomly mask two words or three words out. Then we know the ground truth for those masked words. We can use this as task to pre-train a language model. And similar ideas can be applied to pre-training a vision model. So when we apply this idea of predicting the missing token to images, it's becoming predicting the missing patches.

And the most popular method here is called MAE, or masked auto-encoding. So here we assume we need to process input images. We want to learn some good features from these images. What we do is that we mask out some random patches from input image, then ask the model to reconstruct these missing patches in the output image. And by formulating this task, we can pre-train this computer vision model.

For example, that here we have some input image. So we first need to split this image into a number of patches. Suppose we split it into 14 by 14 equals 196 patches. Then for these 196 patches, we need to mask out some of the patches. We get some image which are shown here on the right hand side. You can see that if It may be even challenging for human to recognize what this object is, but we hope that it still contains some useful features in the input data.

And then we can use this as a very difficult task for the model to learn something. And in technical detail, the master autoencoder follows an encoder-decoder structure. The last time we mentioned about this was about U-Net. We mentioned in U-Net, it follows this symmetric U-shape. On the left-hand side, it's encoder. On the right-hand side, it's decoder. And here, we also use encoder-decoder structure to perform this image reconstruction task.

And we're not using Unet, we're using Vision Transformer. So we assume that the input image, which contains some missing patches, can go through some Vision Transformer. So Vision Transformer can regard the input image as a sequence of tokens, then extract features for each token. And here that for these... This light blue patches mean that the features we learn for each of these visible patches.

And there are also some master patches which are colored using this light gray color. And here, for each of these grayscale patches, it is represented by a special token, which is called the mask tokens. So the features for these light blue patches, they are essentially the features for a patch. So they look like a vector. And for the mask token, we also use a vector to represent the feature.
